comment_text.sn38.1:1.1: Situated near Nāḷandā north of Rājagaha, this village is associated with Sāriputta; the commentary says it was his home town. He speaks there twice with the wanderer Sāmaṇḍakāni (AN 10.65, AN 10.66), and it was the site of his passing (SN 47.13).
comment_text.sn38.1:1.2: Found only in this chapter, his name means “eater of black plums”, perhaps as an ascetic diet (cf. MN 12:52.1). With the exception of the final discourse, all the discourses of this chapter follow the same format, where Jambukhādaka asks for clarification of a central Dhamma term, making the chapter a handy guide to key vocabulary. Several of the terms are found in dependent origination; for explanations of these see SN 12.1 and SN 12.2.
comment_text.sn38.1:2.1: Nibbāna, the going out of a flame, “quenching”.

root_text.sn38.1:1.1: Ekaṁ samayaṁ āyasmā sāriputto magadhesu viharati nālakagāmake.
root_text.sn38.1:1.2: Atha kho jambukhādako paribbājako yenāyasmā sāriputto tenupasaṅkami; upasaṅkamitvā āyasmatā sāriputtena saddhiṁ sammodi.
root_text.sn38.1:1.3: Sammodanīyaṁ kathaṁ sāraṇīyaṁ vītisāretvā ekamantaṁ nisīdi. Ekamantaṁ nisinno kho jambukhādako paribbājako āyasmantaṁ sāriputtaṁ etadavoca:
root_text.sn38.1:2.5: “Atthi panāvuso, maggo atthi paṭipadā etassa nibbānassa sacchikiriyāyā”ti?
root_text.sn38.1:2.6: “Atthi kho, āvuso, maggo atthi paṭipadā etassa nibbānassa sacchikiriyāyā”ti.
root_text.sn38.1:2.7: “Katamo panāvuso, maggo katamā paṭipadā etassa nibbānassa sacchikiriyāyā”ti?
root_text.sn38.1:2.8: “Ayameva kho, āvuso, ariyo aṭṭhaṅgiko maggo etassa nibbānassa sacchikiriyāya, seyyathidaṁ—
root_text.sn38.1:2.9: sammādiṭṭhi sammāsaṅkappo sammāvācā sammākammanto sammāājīvo sammāvāyāmo sammāsati sammāsamādhi.
root_text.sn38.1:2.10: Ayaṁ kho, āvuso, maggo ayaṁ paṭipadā etassa nibbānassa sacchikiriyāyā”ti.
root_text.sn38.1:2.11: “Bhaddako, āvuso, maggo bhaddikā paṭipadā etassa nibbānassa sacchikiriyāya.

translation_text.sn38.1:1.1: At one time Venerable Sāriputta was staying in the land of the Magadhans near the little village of Nālaka.
translation_text.sn38.1:1.2: Then the wanderer Jambukhādaka went up to Venerable Sāriputta and exchanged greetings with him.
translation_text.sn38.1:1.3: When the greetings and polite conversation were over, he sat down to one side and said to Sāriputta:
translation_text.sn38.1:2.1: “Reverend Sāriputta, they speak of this thing called ‘extinguishment’.
translation_text.sn38.1:2.3: “Reverend, the ending of greed, hate, and delusion
translation_text.sn38.1:2.5: “But, reverend, is there a path and a practice for realizing this extinguishment?”
translation_text.sn38.1:2.8: “It is simply this noble eightfold path, that is:
translation_text.sn38.1:2.9: right view, right thought, right speech, right action, right livelihood, right effort, right mindfulness, and right immersion.
translation_text.sn38.1:2.10: This is the path, the practice, for realizing this extinguishment.”
translation_text.sn38.1:2.11: “Reverend, this is a fine path, a fine practice, for realizing this extinguishment.
translation_text.sn38.1:2.12: Just this much is enough for diligence.”
